messages.content: Write a forum convo in mid 2023 where Granada Sky Broadcasting file trademarks for "Kangurola" with the aim to be a younger skewing kids channel, A user assumes this the "Little CITV" strand on CITV will be ending, and that one of the channels will close (probably one of the little watched ones like Granada Breeze or Carlton Select)

**Thread Title**: Granada Sky Broadcasting Files Trademarks for "Kangurola" - End of "Little CITV"?

**CoolTVFan123**: Hey, everyone! Did you see Granada Sky Broadcasting just filed trademarks for something called "Kangurola"? They're positioning it as a younger skewing kids channel. Does this mean we might see the end of the "Little CITV" strand on CITV?

**SkyWatcher88**: Intriguing! I saw the filing. It sounds like they're trying to carve out a new niche. But ending "Little CITV"? That would be a big shift. I know not every strand lasts forever, but "Little CITV" has its fans.

**GranadaGuru**: Honestly, I wouldn't be too quick to assume "Little CITV" is going under. Granada Sky Broadcasting has been expanding its portfolio lately. "Kangurola" could just be an addition rather than a replacement. They have a history of trying to segment their audience.

**ChannelChaser**: What if "Kangurola" is meant to exist alongside "Little CITV"? Maybe it's their strategy to cover broader age groups more effectively. Younger skewing could mean they’re targeting the preschool demographic more aggressively.

**TechSavvy101**: While we’re speculating, let's not forget that Granada or Carlton might close one of their less popular channels to make room for "Kangurola". My bet is on Granada Breeze or Carlton Select. They haven’t been performing well, and it wouldn't surprise me if one of them gets the axe.

**NostalgiaNerd**: Sad to think about Granada Breeze or Carlton Select closing. They've had their moments, even if ratings haven't been stellar. But I guess in the current TV landscape, it’s adapt or die. "Kangurola" could just be the latest in trying to capture those elusive younger audiences in a way that traditional segments haven't.

**FutureTVAnalyst**: Everyone's making good points, but let's also consider streaming and digital platforms. "Kangurola" might not be a traditional TV channel at all. Could be an app or a streaming service aimed at kids. This way, Granada Sky Broadcasting could keep all their current channels and just add "Kangurola" as a digital venture.

**KidShowsForever**: Crossing my fingers that this move doesn’t mean the end for "Little CITV". My kids love that block. Maybe "Kangurola" will add more variety to their viewing choices. As long as the quality doesn’t dip, more options are always welcome.

**RealityCheck**: True, expansion doesn’t always mean replacing what currently exists. Granada Sky has been known to experiment. Remember how they’ve introduced channels and services before, some with great success and others, well, not so much. Let's not jump to conclusions and wait for an official announcement regarding how "Kangurola" will fit into their current lineup.

**AdminNotice**: Great discussion, everyone! It’s important to remember that at this point, much of this is speculation. Let's keep an eye out for any official statements from Granada Sky Broadcasting about "Kangurola" and its impact on their existing channels and offerings. Stay tuned and keep the conversation respectful and informed!
Write a follow up in July where they apply to Ofcom to "reduce Carlton Food Network (Taste CFN) to 7pm-6am for a new channel aimed at young children"

**Thread Title**: **July Update: Carlton Food Network (Taste CFN) Hours Cut for New Kids Channel**

**AdminNotice**: Hello everyone! We’ve got some big news that directly relates to our ongoing discussion about Granada Sky Broadcasting's new venture into children's entertainment with "Kangurola". They've officially applied to Ofcom to reduce the broadcast hours of Carlton Food Network (Taste CFN) to 7pm-6am to make space for a new channel aimed at young children. Let’s dissect this!

**CoolTVFan123**: Wow, so it's happening! This feels like confirmation that "Kangurola" is indeed a channel and not just a digital platform. Cutting down Taste CFN's hours is a big move. I wonder how it will impact their content and viewer base.

**SkyWatcher88**: Interesting development. I did not expect them to reduce Taste CFN's hours. This suggests that "Kangurola" is a pretty significant investment for them. They must have a lot of confidence in this new kids' channel.

**GranadaGuru**: It makes sense from a strategic standpoint. There's a huge demand for children's programming, especially content that’s educational and entertaining. Reducing Taste CFN's airtime to prioritize a potentially more profitable demographic could pay off.

**ChannelChaser**: Feels like a risky move to cut into Taste CFN, though. It's established, has its audience. Are they risking alienating those viewers, or do they assume people will just adapt to the new hours?

**TechSavvy101**: This could also be an indication of how viewing habits have changed. Maybe their data shows that Taste CFN’s audience peaks in the evening and nighttime, which made them comfortable sacrificing daytime broadcasting for "Kangurola".

**NostalgiaNerd**: As someone who enjoyed both Taste CFN and is excited for "Kangurola", I’m torn. It’s always tough to see established programming get cut back, but if "Kangurola" can bring something new and valuable to the table, it could be worth it.

**FutureTVAnalyst**: Let's remember, this is a strategic business decision. Channels evolve, and Granada Sky Broadcasting is adapting to market demands. Kids' programming is lucrative, and if done right, "Kangurola" could be a hit that justifies the reduction of Taste CFN’s airtime.

**KidShowsForever**: As a parent, I’m actually quite excited about "Kangurola". Finding quality content for kids is always a challenge. I just hope they manage to create programming that’s both educational and entertaining. This could be a big win for families.

**RealityCheck**: Amid all these changes, let’s not forget the people behind Taste CFN who might be affected by reduced airtime. Hope there are plans in place to adapt and perhaps integrate some of the food-centric content into "Kangurola" during daytime programming? Could be a way to blend educational content with fun cooking segments for kids.

**AdminNotice**: Great points all around! It's clear that this move by Granada Sky Broadcasting is stirring up a lot of emotions and thoughts. The transition period will be crucial in determining how viewer habits might change and how the audience adapts to "Kangurola". Let’s keep monitoring the situation and share updates as they come. Remember, change always brings new opportunities. Let’s stay open and see how this plays out!
Write the filing, proposing to cut back TCFN starting 1st November, and explain why, also staying that CFN has seen a decline thanks to Food Network and especially YouTube

**Granada Sky Broadcasting(GSB)**   
Attn: Ofcom Licensing Team   
Riverside House   
2a Southwark Bridge Road   
London, SE1 9HA   
United Kingdom   

**Date: July 15, 2023**

Subject: **Proposal to Amend Broadcasting Hours for Carlton Food Network (Taste CFN) with Proposed Introduction of "Kangurola"**

Dear Sir/Madam,

Granada Sky Broadcasting is writing to formally submit a proposal to amend the broadcasting hours of the Carlton Food Network (Taste CFN), a dedicated culinary channel within our broadcasting network, effective from November 1st, 2023. The proposed adjustment will see Taste CFN's broadcasting hours shift to a 7 pm to 6 am schedule, reducing its daytime presence to launch a new initiative aimed at young audiences, tentatively titled "Kangurola."

**Rationale for Adjustment:**

The landscape of culinary programming has experienced significant shifts over the past few years. Taste CFN has been a valued part of our network, providing viewers with a dedicated channel for culinary arts and food entertainment. However, the emergence of digital platforms, particularly YouTube, and specialized networks such as the Food Network, has led to a noticeable fragmentation of our audience. 

Data have highlighted a steady decline in daytime viewership, attributed to the availability of on-demand culinary content accessible on various platforms at the convenience of the viewer. These platforms, especially YouTube, offer a myriad of content from across the globe, catering to niche dietary preferences and diversifying culinary practices, directly impacting the viewer base of Taste CFN.

**Integration of "Kangurola":**

In response to these shifts and in acknowledging the evolving viewer interests, GSB is looking to innovate and diversify its portfolio by introducing "Kangurola." This initiative is conceived as a response to the growing demand for quality, educational, and engaging content tailored for a young audience. "Kangurola" aims to cater to this demographic, providing a blend of educational programming, animated series, and content fostering creativity, health, and well-being among young viewers.

The decision to adjust Taste CFN's broadcasting schedule is aligned with our strategic vision to not only adapt to the changing dynamics of content consumption but also to fulfill our commitment to fostering a diverse and rich viewing experience across all demographics.

**Commitment to Culinary Programming:**

It is imperative to communicate that while Taste CFN's schedule is being revised, GSB remains committed to culinary programming. The scheduled hours are designed to align with peak viewing times, as indicated by our viewership data, to ensure that Taste CFN continues to serve its dedicated audience with the quality content it is renowned for. 

Additionally, we are exploring multipronged strategies to integrate culinary content within "Kangurola," envisaging segments that promote nutritional education, culinary skills for children, and family cooking shows, thereby blending both educational values and culinary arts into children’s programming.

**Conclusion:**

Granada Sky Broadcasting believes that this strategic adjustment will not only address the challenges faced by Taste CFN in the current content consumption landscape but will also pave the way for "Kangurola" to emerge as a pioneering channel dedicated to young audiences.

We anticipate your approval of this proposal and are available to provide any further information or clarification required. 

Yours sincerely,

[Name]  
[Position]  
Granada Sky Broadcasting

Write a convo where the look of the channel is revealed, pink blobs morphing into the shape of kangaroos, Also the preschool block on CITV is ending from 1st January, ending the blue background version of the "Stretchy" bumpers, (Big kids content is red background, preschool is blue)

**Thread Title:** New Look for "Kangurola" and The End of CITV's Preschool Block?

**JumpingJellyBeans:** Hey everyone, have you seen the latest reveal on "Kangurola"? The channel's aesthetic is taking a fun twist with pink blobs morphing into kangaroos. It's quirky and very eye-catching. Thoughts?

**PixelParent:** Just caught that! The pink blobs are a genius idea; it's whimsical and should catch the little ones' attention. As for the kangaroos, they're a perfect mascot for a channel aimed at younger kids. It's playful and aligns well with the adventurous spirit they're probably aiming for.

**NostalgiaKnight:** I’m intrigued by the design choice. It’s definitely a departure from the norm. But on a different note, did you guys also read about the preschool block on CITV ending this January? It seems they’re retiring the blue background "Stretchy" bumpers. I’ll miss those.

**TellyTales:** Yeah, @NostalgiaKnight, I saw that about CITV's preschool block. It’s kinda sad. Those blue "Stretchy" bumpers were iconic. But it makes sense with "Kangurola" coming in. Looks like they’re consolidating their young kids' content under one umbrella. Still, an end of an era.

**DigitalDad:** It's a smart move, honestly. The market’s super competitive, and having a specialized channel like "Kangurola" could give them an edge. The end of CITV’s preschool block is bittersweet, but evolution in kids' TV is inevitable, I guess. 

**CreativeCritic:** While I'm excited for "Kangurola" and its innovative approach, part of me wonders how they'll stand out with so much digital content out there. YouTube and apps are dominating the space. The pink blobs to kangaroos is memorable, but they'll need solid content to back up the cool visuals.

**KiddieContentConnoisseur:** Agreed, @CreativeCritic. The aesthetics are one thing, but I’m eagerly waiting to see what kinds of shows "Kangurola" will offer. As for CITV phasing out the preschool segment, perhaps it’s a strategic retreat to focus energies on making "Kangurola" a success.

**TechieMom:** It does feel like an end of an era with the CITV changes, but also the beginning of a potentially exciting new chapter. If "Kangurola" manages to balance educational content with fun, it could easily become a staple for kids' morning routines. Very curious about the programming lineup now.

**AnimationAficionado:** Does anyone know if "Kangurola" plans to include original animations, or will it be more of an acquisitions-heavy channel? The unique branding suggests they're aiming for something fresh and creative, which hopefully means new and original content.

**JumpingJellyBeans:** There’s not much out there yet about the programming, @AnimationAficionado, but given the effort they're putting into branding, I'm optimistic about original content. Also, considering they’re filling the gap left by CITV’s preschool block, there's a lot of potentials to explore new genres and formats.

**PixelParent:** Regardless, it's definitely a time of change. I’ll miss the old CITV mornings but am also genuinely excited to see what "Kangurola" brings to the table. Keeping an open mind and hoping for the best!

**AdminNote:** Great discussion, everyone! Remember to keep an eye on official announcements for more details about "Kangurola" and its offerings. Let’s continue to support children’s programming and nurture the love of storytelling in the next generation!
Write a follow up asking how Carlton Cinema, Sport, World, Select and Granada Plus are doing

**Thread Title:** How Are Carlton and Granada's Niche Channels Faring in the Current Television Landscape?

**AdminPost:** Hello, TV enthusiasts! In light of the significant changes and developments with Granada Sky Broadcasting's children's programming and the strategy shifts in their scheduling, I thought it'd be interesting to turn our attention to the other niche channels under the Carlton and Granada banners. Specifically, we're looking at Carlton Cinema, Carlton Sport, Carlton World, Carlton Select, and Granada Plus. Given the evolving media consumption habits and the stiff competition from streaming platforms, how are these channels doing? Let’s dive in!

**ClassicFilmFan:** Has anyone watched Carlton Cinema lately? It used to be my go-to for classic movies, but I've found myself drifting towards streaming services for my old movie fix. Do they still have a robust lineup?

**SportsWatcher:** Carlton Sport seems to be holding its own, especially with the coverage of less mainstream sports. It’s a niche that’s not fully exploited by major sports networks or streaming services, which could be why they're maintaining their viewership.

**WorldlyViewer:** Carlton World is an interesting case. It’s always had a mix of travel, culture, and world news, but with the rise of YouTube bloggers and specialized streaming content, I wonder if they are struggling to keep the audience engaged?

**SelectSpectator:** I've noticed a shift in Carlton Select’s programming, leaning a bit more towards reality and lifestyle shows. It seems like a strategy to capture viewers from similar genres on streaming platforms. Not sure how successful it's been, though.

**GranadaPlusFan:** Granada Plus is still my favorite for classic TV shows. There's just something nostalgic about flipping through channels and stumbling upon an old favorite series. However, I do admit, with streaming platforms offering the same shows on demand, it's more about the experience than the content availability.

**TechTrendWatcher:** The big challenge for these channels is the profound shift towards on-demand, personalized viewing experiences. Despite this, niche channels have a unique value proposition by catering to specific interests not widely covered on major platforms. The real question is, are they innovating enough to keep up with the times?

**NostalgiaSeeker:** A lot of my friends have cut the cord and rely solely on streaming, but there's a segment of viewers who still appreciate the curated experience these channels offer. Maybe it’s the simplicity, the nostalgia, or just the joy of channel surfing. There's still a place for them, albeit perhaps a shrinking one.

**DigitalNomad:** Could these channels benefit from hybrid models? Offering linear programming but also providing an on-demand streaming service for their niche content could be the way forward. Has there been any talk about something like this for Carlton Cinema or Granada Plus?

**AdminPost:** These insights are fascinating! It's clear there's still affection and curiosity about how these channels are navigating the changing media landscape. As streaming platforms continue to dominate, niche channels need to adapt and perhaps reinvent to stay relevant. Whether through hybrid distribution models, unique content offerings, or tapping into nostalgia, there's no one-size-fits-all solution. Let’s keep this conversation going and see how these channels evolve!

What do you think? Do you have any news, updates, or predictions about the future of Carlton and Granada's niche channels? Share your thoughts!

Write a follow up where all the channel's branding (bar CITV and Kangurola) are the same as in the early 00s, and a user assumes the rubber pres for CITV and the Gloopy pres for Kangurola will also last 20 years

**Thread Title:** Will CITV & Kangurola's Branding Follow the 20-Year Trend?

**RetroTVFan**: Hey all! Have you noticed? Almost all Carlton and Granada channels are still embracing their early 00s branding. It’s like a trip back in time every time you turn on the TV, except for the newer faces like CITV and Kangurola, of course. This got me thinking, with the rubber pres for CITV and the Gloopy pres for Kangurola, are we looking at another 20-year branding lifecycle? 

**NostalgicViewer**: True! The early 00s aesthetic feels like a comfort blanket in today’s fast-paced media landscape. But considering how quickly kids' preferences evolve, can CITV and Kangurola realistically stick to the same branding for two decades? I mean, I love the Gloopy pres for Kangurola; it’s fresh and fun. Yet, 20 years is a long time in the world of children’s TV.

**DigitalDynamo**: It's an interesting point @NostalgicViewer. Brand longevity in kid's channels has to balance consistency with the ability to adapt. The rubber pres for CITV feels dynamic enough to evolve without a complete overhaul. As for Kangurola's Gloopy pres, its playful vibe has a charm that could stand the test of time, yet I suspect iterative updates will be key to keeping it relevant.

**EraEccentric**: Let's not forget the power of nostalgia, especially for parents who might be introducing their kids to these channels. The branding being similar to early 00s for other channels capitalizes on that. Yet, children's channels have a unique challenge; their primary audience is always changing as kids grow up. So, while embracing nostalgia, CITV and Kangurola need to continuously innovate to stay appealing to new generations.

**TrendTracker**: Exactly, @EraEccentric! And considering how technology and media consumption habits are evolving, CITV and Kangurola might need to adapt faster than their predecessors. The transition from linear TV to on-demand digital content is already influencing how brands interact with their audience. Twenty years from now, we might reminisce about the Gloopy pres the way we do about early 00s branding — with fondness but aware of its place in time.

**MediaMaven**: Here’s a thought - what if the brands evolve not by changing their core identity (like the Gloopy pres) but by expanding how and where they engage with their audience? Think augmented reality experiences, interactive content, and deeper integration with social media. The essence of the brand remains, but its expression diversifies to meet viewers wherever they are.

**RetroTVFan**: @MediaMaven, that’s a forward-thinking approach. Preserving the core elements that make CITV and Kangurola's branding unique, while seamlessly integrating modern technology, could indeed make their branding resilient enough to last decades. It’s not just about the visual identity but the experiences and memories those visuals evoke.

**AdminNote**: Fascinating discussion, everyone! Brand longevity, especially in children's entertainment, is a multifaceted challenge. As CITV and Kangurola navigate the fine line between tradition and innovation, it'll be interesting to see how their branding evolves. Will the rubber and Gloopy pres be as iconic in 20 years as the early 00s branding is today? Only time will tell, but for now, let’s enjoy the nostalgia and look forward to the future of children’s programming. Keep the conversation going!
Write a convo asking what branding the new channel has, and a user says its gooey blobs, usually morphing into a kangaroo

**Thread Title:** Curious About the New "Kangurola" Channel's Branding

**StarGazer101**: Hey folks! I've been hearing a lot about the upcoming "Kangurola" channel targeted at the younger audience, but I haven't caught any specifics about its branding. Does anyone here know what visual theme or branding style they're going for?

**PixelPioneer**: Yeah! I got a glimpse of their promotional material recently. The branding for "Kangurola" is quite unique and playful - they're using gooey blobs as a central theme. These blobs often morph into various shapes, but most notably, they transform into kangaroos. It’s very dynamic and seems like it’ll really appeal to kids with its colorful and fluid animation style.

**VisualVibes**: @PixelPioneer, that sounds fascinating! Using such a fluid and engaging motif like gooey blobs definitely sets a tone that both entertains and captures the imagination. It reflects a brand identity that's flexible and fun. Did they reveal any particular color scheme or is it a mix?

**PixelPioneer**: @VisualVibes, from what I've seen, they’re leaning heavily into bright and vibrant colors. Think along the lines of purples, pinks, and greens. It’s not just the morphing gooey blobs but also the way those colors mix and interact that adds a layer of whimsy to their branding. 

**CreativeCrafter**: The idea of morphing blobs is super engaging for developing minds. It's not just about the visuals but also about sparking creativity in kids, imagining what shapes the blobs will take next. Using a kangaroo aligns brilliantly with the idea of jumping around - energetic and always in motion. It provides a subtle nod to staying active, even if it's through playful imagery.

**TechTalker**: As someone interested in the technical side, I'm curious about the animation techniques they're using to create these gooey blobs and morphing effects. It sounds like there’s a significant investment in creating high-quality, visually appealing content that stands out in today’s saturated kids' programming space.

**AnimationApprentice**: @TechTalker, definitely! The technology behind such fluid animations has come a long way. It’s probably a combination of traditional animation principles applied with modern software that allows for such smooth transformations. It would be interesting to find out more about the behind-the-scenes work.

**ChannelChaser**: I wonder how they plan to integrate this visual branding across their programming. Will the morphing gooey blobs be part of show intros, or maybe as transitions between shows? The potential for creating an immersive and cohesive brand experience is huge.

**PixelPioneer**: @ChannelChaser, good point! From the promotional material, it seems like the gooey blobs and kangaroo theme will be a recurring motif, likely serving as transitions and possibly even playing a part in the channel's interactive elements. It gives "Kangurola" a mascot-like character, which can significantly bolster brand recognition among its young audience.

**AdminNote**: This has been a really insightful discussion about the "Kangurola" channel's branding strategy! Thanks for sharing your thoughts and observations. As the launch date approaches, let's keep an eye out for more teasers and promotional materials. It's exciting to see innovative branding come to life, especially in children’s programming. Keep the conversation going and share any new info you come across!

Write a convo in May 2023 where Taste CFN still uses the 2001 idents (white background with colorful beads forming food), Plus still uses the 2004 idents (a family, playing around with a teal ball with a + symbol on it), etc Only the 2 kids channels have looks that originated in the 2020s

**Thread Title: Blast from the Past: Taste CFN and Plus Idents Still Rocking Their Early 2000s Look**

**RetroWave33**: Hey, has anyone else realized that Taste CFN is still using those 2001 idents? You know, the ones with the white background and colorful beads forming different kinds of food. It's like a time capsule every time I switch over!

**ClassicTVFan**: Oh yeah, RetroWave33! And don’t forget about Plus. They’re still airing the 2004 idents with the family playing around with that teal ball with the + symbol on it. It’s kind of wild considering how much everything else in broadcasting has changed.

**NostalgicNerd**: It’s fascinating, isn’t it? While I appreciate a good dose of nostalgia, you have to wonder why they haven’t updated their branding. Especially since the visuals of television have evolved so much, even the intro graphics for news segments are more dynamic now.

**ModernViewer**: I find it interesting contrasted with the two kids' channels, which have looks that are distinctly from the 2020s. Do you think it’s a strategic move to keep the older channels’ branding rooted in nostalgia while pushing forward with more contemporary designs for younger audiences?

**DigitalDiva**: @ModernViewer, that’s an excellent point. There’s definitely a strategy in targeting demographics. Kids and teens today probably wouldn't connect with the 2000s aesthetics, so it makes sense for kids' channels to have more modern visuals. Adults, on the other hand, might appreciate the nostalgia and continuity in branding.

**VisionaryVic**: I wonder if there’s also an element of “if it isn’t broke, don’t fix it” at play. Those idents have become so iconic over the years; they’re almost part of the channel’s identity now. Maybe they worry that changing them might alienate their established audience.

**TechTrendsetter**: There’s merit in nostalgia, but channels also run the risk of appearing outdated if they cling too much to the past. It's a delicate balance. The question becomes: how can they refresh their brand without losing the essence of what made it memorable?

**MemoryLane**: Personally, I adore the old idents on Taste CFN and Plus. They remind me of simpler times. It’s comforting in a way, amidst all the rapid changes and the push for the latest and greatest in every other aspect of media.

**BrandBuilder**: It's all about branding consistency, but also knowing when to evolve. Maybe it's time for these channels to consider a soft rebrand, introducing new elements that complement the existing feel. That way, they refresh their image but still nod to their legacy.

**Admin**: Fascinating discussion, everyone! It’s clear that both the nostalgia of old idents and the excitement of new branding strategies offer much to think about in the landscape of television. It’ll be interesting to see how Taste CFN and Plus navigate their branding in the future. Keep the observations and discussions coming!
Write a tweet with the oldest GSB branding and the newest

🌟Flashback vs. Future🌈: Dive into the #GSBheritage journey from our oldest branding – the classic 2001 Taste CFN idents with their minimalist white background and colorful beads making culinary art, to the vibrant and dynamic world of "Kangurola" in 2023, where gooey blobs morph into playful kangaroos! 🍽️➡️🦘#GSBEvolution #ThrowbackThursday #BrandNewDay
Write a forum convo wondering if Carlton Sport still has the pres from when it launched (2007)

**Thread Title: Does Carlton Sport still rock its original 2007 pres?**

**SportySpeculator**: Hey everyone! Been flipping through channels and landed on Carlton Sport. Got me wondering, are they still running with the presentation (pres) graphics from when they first launched back in 2007? It feels like I’ve time-traveled back to when HD was the new big thing. Anyone else noticed?

**VintageVibes**: Yeah, @SportySpeculator, I've noticed that too! It seems like they've kept a lot of the original aesthetic. It's pretty nostalgic. But with the way technology's evolved, it's surprising they haven't opted for a more modern makeover. That early 2000s look is unmistakable.

**TechTrendWatcher**: You're both right. It appears Carlton Sport has kept its commitment to the 2007 pres style. It's an interesting choice in a world where channels are pushing the envelope with 4K and even 8K presentations. Makes you wonder if it’s a budget thing or just sticking with what works for their audience.

**GraphicGuru**: From a design perspective, there's something to be said about maintaining a consistent brand image over the years. Though, the 2007 graphics do stand out as a bit dated in today's landscape. It would be interesting to see how they could modernize those visuals without losing the essence of their original branding.

**NostalgiaNut**: I must admit, every time I catch a game on Carlton Sport, the old-school graphics give me a sense of comfort. Like visiting an old friend. But I wouldn’t be opposed to a refresh. They could keep the core elements but bring the presentation into the 2020s.

**DigitalDynamo**: Exactly, @NostalgiaNut! There’s a way to evolve the branding where it maintains its original charm but leverages new tech for a sharper, more contemporary look. A fine balance, but definitely doable. It's all about not alienating the long-time viewers while attracting new ones.

**MediaMaven**: Does anyone know if they have plans to update it? With all the competition out there, I'd think Carlton Sport would want to spruce things up to stay relevant. Plus, with emerging tech, they could create some truly immersive viewing experiences.

**RetroReviver**: No updates that I’ve heard of, @MediaMaven. Seems like they’re sticking to their guns for now. But with all the changes in how we consume sports content nowadays, it wouldn’t surprise me if there was a shake-up on the horizon to keep pace.

**AdminNote**: Great discussion, everyone! It’s clear that Carlton Sport’s loyalty to their original pres from 2007 has sparked nostalgia, debate, and speculation. It’s a testament to the impact of branding and presentation in the sports broadcasting world. Keep the insights coming and let’s stay tuned to see if Carlton Sport decides to leap into the present day with their graphics.
Write a convo wondering what's happened to Wellbeing (Granada's health channel), and a user said it did a low key closure last night, and now it's a +1 feed of K/TCFN

**Thread Title: The Quiet Goodbye of Wellbeing - Granada’s Health Channel**

**HealthHive**: Hey everyone, I was flipping through the channels this morning, expecting to catch my regular wellness updates on Wellbeing, Granada's dedicated health channel, and instead, I found something else. It seems like Wellbeing isn't broadcasting its unique content anymore. Does anyone know what happened?

**DigitalDoc**: I heard some whispers about this but didn’t want to believe it until I saw it for myself. Wellbeing did indeed undergo a very low-key closure last night. There wasn’t much fanfare or an official announcement, at least none that I caught. Now, it’s simply operating as a +1 feed of K/TCFN. Quite a shift from dedicated health content to a time-shift of another channel.

**MindfulMedia**: That’s so disappointing! Wellbeing was a treasure trove of health and wellness programming. It’s surprising they would shut it down so quietly, especially considering the current global emphasis on health and wellness. Transitioning it to just another +1 feed feels like a missed opportunity.

**StreamSleuth**: Honestly, I didn’t see much promotion or support for Wellbeing in recent times. It seems like it could be a strategic decision, perhaps due to viewership numbers or the cost of producing specialized content. Rerunning K/TCFN content as a +1 feed is likely a cost-effective measure. Still, it's sad to see it go without so much as a nod to its audiences.

**FitnessFanatic**: Such a bummer! I had a routine of starting my mornings with their yoga and mindfulness sessions. It’s like losing a part of my daily routine. I wish they had at least given the channel a proper send-off or transitioned its content online. It seems abrupt to switch to a +1 feed with no heads up.

**ChannelChecker**: I'm not entirely surprised by the shift. In the digital age, niche channels like Wellbeing face tough competition from online platforms and social media, where health content is abundant and free. Maybe they thought the +1 feed of K/TCFN would reach a broader audience somehow.

**TechTalker**: @FitnessFanatic, I totally get where you're coming from. It might be worthwhile to reach out to Granada or explore their online platforms—sometimes content gets shifted to digital formats. As for the low-key closure, it seems like a sign of the times; linear TV is facing more challenges, and silent closures might become more common, unfortunately.

**AdminAnnouncement**: It’s clear the closure of Wellbeing has left a void for those who relied on its content for health and wellness inspiration. While the channel may be gone, the community it built and the interest in health-focused programming remain strong. Let’s continue to share resources, updates, and maybe even find alternatives that can help fill the gap Wellbeing has left behind. Your passion for health and wellness keeps the spirit of Wellbeing alive in this community.
Write a convo the night before saying Wellbeing closes at 7am and say the 2 channels it will become a +1 feed of

**Thread Title: Farewell to Wellbeing: Closing Tomorrow at 7 AM**

**ZenGuru**: Just caught wind of some sad news. It looks like we’re bidding farewell to Wellbeing, folks. 🌿 Official word is it’s closing down at 7 am tomorrow. After that, it's transitioning to a +1 feed of both Kangurola and Taste CFN (TCFN). Definitely the end of an era.

**HealthVibes**: Seriously? That’s incredibly disappointing. Wellbeing had such a unique lineup of health and wellness programming. It’s a real loss to see it go. Do we know why they decided to shut it down? Seems quite sudden.

**DigitalDoc**: @HealthVibes, from what I’ve gathered, it’s likely due to the strategic reshuffling within the network. Consolidation seems to be a trend, with a focus on channels that can pull in broader viewerships. A +1 feed for Kangurola and TCFN probably fits better into their wider strategy, even though it’s at the cost of niche content like what Wellbeing offered.

**MorningYogi**: This is a heartbreak! 😢 My mornings won’t be the same without my Wellbeing routine. It felt like one of the few channels genuinely dedicated to health and wellness. Now, being replaced by a +1 of Kangurola and TCFN just doesn’t fill that gap.

**StreamSleuth**: It’s a tough time for specialized content on linear TV. With the rise of on-demand and streaming platforms, these niche channels are finding it harder to compete. While I understand the business decision, it’s a shame the transition couldn't include a digital platform for Wellbeing content.

**FitnessFanatic**: Anyone planning a final viewing party? We should send Wellbeing off with a bang – or at least a deep, mindful breath. It’s been such a resource for those of us trying to maintain a healthy lifestyle. Sad to see it reduced to a +1 feed.

**TechTalker**: It’s definitely sad to see it go. Might be worth reaching out to the network or following their social media for any announcements on whether Wellbeing’s content might be made available elsewhere. Sometimes these closures pave the way for digital-only content. Here’s hoping 🤞.

**EcoEcho**: This feels like a significant loss, especially in times when health and wellness are so front and center in people’s minds. Transitioning Wellbeing to just a +1 feed of Kangurola and TCFN feels shortsighted. Surely, there was a way to integrate its content into the broader channels’ lineups instead.

**AdminNotice**: It’s heartening to see such a strong community response to the news of Wellbeing’s closure. As we count down the final hours, let’s celebrate the positive impact it’s had on our lives. Share your favorite Wellbeing moments, and let’s keep the conversation going about health and wellness in all its forms. Remember, while the channel may be closing, the journey to well-being continues.
